coredata.dc:title: Effects of Li ion-solvent interaction on ionic transport and electrochemical properties in highly concentrated cyclic carbonate electrolytes
coredata.dc:creator: Shigenobu, Keisuke
Sudoh, Taku
Tabuchi, Mayu
Tsuzuki, Seiji
Shinoda, Wataru
Dokko, Kaoru
Watanabe, Masayoshi
Ueno, Kazuhide
coredata.dc:description: In recent research, the importance of electrolytes with high Li+ transference number (t Li) and ionic conductivity (σ ion) has been emphasized to realize rapid charge for Li secondary batteries. Simultaneously fulfilling high t Li and σ ion is still unsolved in liquid electrolytes; however, highly concentrated electrolytes (HCEs) of weakly coordinating solvents and Li salts will be promising for addressing this challenge. This idea is inspired by a recent study by Angell et al. on superprotonic ionic liquids comprising a weak Brønsted base and a superacid; highly labile and exchangeable H+ can be formed between significantly weak proton accepting sites. Here, we studied weakly coordinating fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC)-based electrolytes with lithium bis(fluorosulfonyl)amide (Li[FSA]) and compared with ethylene carbonate (EC)-based electrolytes. Experimental and computational studies indicated that solvent and ion exchange is more pronounced in the FEC-based HCE, resulting in higher t Li PP (0.73) and ionic conductivity (1.02 mS cm−1) compared to those of the EC-based HCE (t Li PP= 0.53 and σ ion= 0.84 mS cm−1). However, the FEC-based HCE exhibited lower electrochemical stability due to the intrinsically lower reductive stability of FEC and the oxidative decomposition of the liberated solvent in the HCE. Despite the superior transport properties, the Li/LiCoO2 cell with the FEC-based electrolyte showed lower discharge capacities and lower Coulombic efficiencies at higher current densities due to side reactions of the electrolyte. This study demonstrates that weak Li-solvent interactions can simultaneously enhance t Li and σ ion of HCEs, but they have the potential to sacrifice the electrochemical stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Highly concentrated electrolyte
Lithium transference number
Ionic conductivity
Fluoroethylene carbonate
Weakly coordinating property.

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: "Glass is frozen beauty" - A Memorial Issue in Honor of C. Austen Angell (1933 – 2021)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Computational Methods (ab initio calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations)
t Li PP and Onsager transport coefficients

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported in part by the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) KAKENHI (Grant No. 20H02837 to K. U. 18H03926 to S. T. and 18H03926 to K. D.), JST ALCA-SPRING (Grant No. JPMJAL1301) and Asahi Glass Foundation, Japan. This study is also based on results obtained from a project, JPNP20004, subsidized by the New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization (NEDO).

This study was supported in part by the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) KAKENHI (Grant No. 20H02837 to K. U., 18H03926 to S. T., and 18H03926 to K. D.), JST ALCA-SPRING (Grant No. JPMJAL1301 ) and Asahi Glass Foundation, Japan . This study is also based on results obtained from a project, JPNP20004 , subsidized by the New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization (NEDO) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The MP2/6-311G** level optimised geometries and intermolecular interaction energies (E int) for (a) [Li(EC)]+ and (b) [Li(FEC)]+. Energy (kcal mol−1).

Normalized Raman spectra of (a) EC and EC-based electrolytes and (b) FEC and FEC-based electrolytes in the range from 1200 to 1260 cm−1 at 30 °C except EC, [Li(EC)50][FSA], and [Li(EC)30][FSA], and they were measured at 40 °C.

Radial distribution function (solid line) and accumulated coordination number (dashed line) between Li+ and oxygen atoms in solvents (EC or FEC) (black) and [FSA]− (red). (a) [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and (b) [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Cyclic voltammograms obtained for (a) [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and (b) [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] on Cu electrode at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1 30 °C.

Linear sweep voltammograms obtained for [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] (black) and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] (red) in the range 4–6 V on a Pt electrode at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1 and at 30 °C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The cycle performance of discharge capacity with different current densities ranging from 0.4 mA cm−2 to 5 mA cm−2 and discharge curves of the cells with (b) [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and (c) [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] at 30 °C.
δ 23Na, DN NMR and dielectric constant (ε r) of EC and FEC.
Stabilisation energy calculated for formation of Li+ complexes with ethylene carbonate (EC) and fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) and contributions of electrostatic and induction interactions a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Energy in kcal mol−1. Geometries of the complexes are shown in Fig. 1.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Stabilization energy by the formation of complexes from isolated species. Sum of E int and E def.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The E int is the calculated interaction energy at the MP2/6-311G** level.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The increase in energy of EC or FEC by the deformation of geometry associated with complex formation. See text.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: g
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: E other = E int − E es − E ind. E other is mainly exchange-repulsion and dispersion energies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Viscosity (η), Li[FSA] concentration (c), ionic conductivity (σ), and diffusivity data of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] at 30 °C.
Self-diffusion coefficients of Li, [FSA] and solvents (EC and FEC) in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] at 403 K calculated by MD simulations.
t Li PP, α and β of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA].

head.ce:title: Effects of Li ion-solvent interaction on ionic transport and electrochemical properties in highly concentrated cyclic carbonate electrolytes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent research, the importance of electrolytes with high Li+ transference number (t Li) and ionic conductivity (σ ion) has been emphasized to realize rapid charge for Li secondary batteries. Simultaneously fulfilling high t Li and σ ion is still unsolved in liquid electrolytes; however, highly concentrated electrolytes (HCEs) of weakly coordinating solvents and Li salts will be promising for addressing this challenge. This idea is inspired by a recent study by Angell et al. on superprotonic ionic liquids comprising a weak Brønsted base and a superacid; highly labile and exchangeable H+ can be formed between significantly weak proton accepting sites. Here, we studied weakly coordinating fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC)-based electrolytes with lithium bis(fluorosulfonyl)amide (Li[FSA]) and compared with ethylene carbonate (EC)-based electrolytes. Experimental and computational studies indicated that solvent and ion exchange is more pronounced in the FEC-based HCE, resulting in higher t Li PP (0.73) and ionic conductivity (1.02 mS cm−1) compared to those of the EC-based HCE (t Li PP= 0.53 and σ ion= 0.84 mS cm−1). However, the FEC-based HCE exhibited lower electrochemical stability due to the intrinsically lower reductive stability of FEC and the oxidative decomposition of the liberated solvent in the HCE. Despite the superior transport properties, the Li/LiCoO2 cell with the FEC-based electrolyte showed lower discharge capacities and lower Coulombic efficiencies at higher current densities due to side reactions of the electrolyte. This study demonstrates that weak Li-solvent interactions can simultaneously enhance t Li and σ ion of HCEs, but they have the potential to sacrifice the electrochemical stability.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of electrolyte solutions for Li secondary batteries has attracted significant interest over the past decades since it is essential for achieving better battery performance such as rapid charge capability and cycle stability. In particular, the feasibility of rapid charge capability in Li secondary batteries is inseparably related to the Li transport properties of the electrolytes such as the ionic conductivity and Li+ transference number. Conventional organic electrolytes have frequently been employed with a salt concentration of 1 mol dm−3 because of their maximum ionic conductivity; however, they have a low Li+ transference number (< 0.4) [1]. Recently, highly concentrated electrolytes (HCEs) have been considered promising liquid electrolytes owing to their fascinating properties such as high thermal, electrochemical and long-term cycle stability, suppression of Al corrosion and high rate capability [2–9]. In terms of the Li+ transport properties, sulfolane-based HCEs show a unique Li+ hopping/exchanging conduction that contributes to a high Li+ transference number (~ 0.6) and enhanced rate capability [10,11]. Moreover, recent research has suggested that achieving a high Li+ transference number is essential for the rapid charging and suppression of Li dendrite formation; the improvement of the Li+ transference number has been considered a crucial issue for liquid electrolytes [12–14].

Transport properties of HCEs has been systematically analyzed considering the ion motions based on the concentrated solution theory. Bedrov and Roling have demonstrated computational and experimental analysis of ion transport in ether-based electrolytes by utilizing the Onsager transport coefficients, where the ionic conductivity, σ ion, is given as σ ion = σ ++ + σ −− − 2σ +− with the contribution of cations (σ ++), anions (σ −−) and the cation-anion (σ +−) correlation to the total ionic conductivity (see Supplementary Information (SI)) [15–17]. In a previous study, we investigated the effects of solvents and anions on the transport properties of ether- and sulfolane-based HCEs from the viewpoint of the dynamic ion correlations based on the Onsager transport coefficients, σ ++, σ −− and σ +− [18,19]. The coefficients can also be linked to the Li+ transference number under anion-blocking conditions (t Li PP) using the parameters α and β [15,16]:
(1)
t Li PP
=
β 2
− 4 α + 4
α 2
4
1 − α
β − 1
(2)
α =
σ
+ +
σ
+ +
+
σ
− −
, β =
2
σ
+ −
σ
+ +
+
σ
− −
where α is related to the extent to which Li+ contributes to ionic conduction and β strongly depends on the cation-anion correlation. The significantly high t Li PP value (~ 0.7) of the sulfolane-based HCEs was ascribed to less anti-correlated cation-cation and cation-anion motions with α= 0.9 and β= −0.2 [18,20].

Our previous study proposed that electrolytes with strongly correlated cation–cation ion motions (α≈1) and non-correlated cation–anion ion motions (β≈0) would be ideal for achieving a high Li+ transference number as well as high ionic conductivity [19]. One possible approach is to stimulate solvent motions in HCEs which can reduce the anti-correlations of the ion motions while satisfying momentum conservation [15]. In a recent study by Angell et al., superionic H+ transport was accomplished for a protic ionic liquid comprising weakly basic pentafluoropyridine (C5F5N) and strongly acidic HAlCl4, in which highly labile H+ can be formed between significantly weak proton accepting-sites (i.e., C5F5N and AlCl4 −) [21]. In this study, a weakly coordinating aprotic solvent, fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC), was adopted for HCEs in anticipation of its highly mobile solvent motions and the formation of more labile Li+ in the HCE. The coordination structures of Li+, ionic transport, electrochemical properties and battery performance of FEC-based HCEs were compared with those of ethylene carbonate (EC)-based HCEs to examine the effects of interactions between Li ions and weakly coordinating solvents.

Battery grade solvents, namely, purified ethylene carbonate (EC), fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) and a battery grade Li salt, lithium bis(fluorosulfonyl)amide (Li[FSA]) were purchased from Kishida Chemical Co. (Japan) and used as received. HCEs, [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] were prepared at the solvent/Li[FSA] molar ratio of 1.5 in an inert argon-filled glove box ([H2O] <1 ppm; [O2] <1 ppm).

Ionic conductivities (σ ion) were obtained using the complex impedance method for frequency between 500 kHz and 1 Hz at an alternating voltage amplitude of 100 mV (VMP3, Biologic). The cell constants of the conductivity cells (two platinum black electrode cells) were determined using a 0.01 mol dm−3 KCl aqueous solution at 25 °C before the measurements. The viscosities and densities (Table S1) of both electrolytes were simultaneously measured using a Stabinger viscometer (SVM300, Anton Paar). The self-diffusion coefficients of Li, [FSA] and solvents were estimated via pulse field gradient (PFG)–NMR measurement using a bipolar pulse-pair longitudinal eddy current delay (BPP–LED) pulse sequence [22].

Donor numbers (DN NMR) of the solvents were calculated using the empirical formula shown below [23] (Eq. (3)):
(3)
DN NMR
= 32 + 2.1 ×
δ
23 Na
where δ 23Na is the experimentally obtained chemical shift of 23Na, which was collected by conducting 23Na NMR measurements. We utilized a coaxial NMR tube; the reference standard, 3 mol dm−3 NaCl in D2O, was inserted into the inner tube and the samples were injected into the outer tube with the dissolution of NaClO4 in the intended solvents at 0.4 mol dm−3.

Dielectric constants (ε r) of the solvents were calculated from the electrostatic capacity of capacitors obtained from the complex capacitance method in the frequency range of 50 Hz–500 kHz at an alternating voltage amplitude of 500 mV (VMP3, Biologic) at 30 °C. Super dehydrated ethanol, methanol and tetrahydrofuran were used as the reference standards for measuring the dielectric constants. Reference standards and samples were injected into a sample holder that comprised two gold electrodes (SH2-Z type four terminals sample holder, TOYO Corporation). Cell constants were determined by measuring the electrostatic capacity of the capacitors using the reference standards.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) were performed on a potentiostat (VMP3, Biologic) using a three-electrode cell comprising a copper disk electrode (diameter = ~3 mm) for CV and a platinum disk electrode (diameter = ~3 mm) for LSV as the working electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode and lithium metal soaked in 1 mol dm−3 Li[TFSA] in propylene carbonate (PC) as the reference electrode. The Li/Li+ reference electrode was separated from the sample electrolyte by a liquid junction with a Vycor glass frit.

Charge–discharge tests were conducted using R2032-type cells under a [Li | electrolyte | LiCoO2]. Li (diameter = 16 mm) foil and an LiCoO2 (LCO) electrode (0.64 mAh cm−2, diameter = 16 mm) were applied as the negative and positive electrodes, respectively. The LCO electrode was prepared following our previous paper [24], and the loading capacity was set to 0.64 mAh cm−2 to compare the rate capability. Two separator sheets (Celgard3501, Celgard, diameter = 17 mm) were placed between the electrodes to retain the electrolytes. The cells were assembled in an inert Ar-filled glove box under low oxygen and moisture levels (O2 and H2O < 0.5 ppm). Galvanostatic charge–discharge tests were performed on an automatic charge and discharge instrument (HJ1001SM8A, Hokuto Denko) at 30 °C and the cut-off voltages were set at 3.0 V and 4.2 V for the discharge and charge steps, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational Methods (ab initio calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations)
ce:section.ce:para: Ab initio molecular orbital calculations were conducted using the Gaussian 16 program [25]. The geometries of the EC, FEC, [Li(EC)]+ and [Li(FEC)]+ complexes were optimized at the MP2/6-311G** level. The intermolecular interaction energies (E int) for the [Li(EC)]+ and [Li(FEC)]+ complexes were calculated at the MP2/6-311G** level using supermolecule method [26]. The basis set superposition error (BSSE) [27] was corrected by the counterpoise method [28] for the interaction energy calculations. HOMOs and LUMOs were also calculated at the MP2/6-311G** level. The stabilization energy for forming a complex from the isolated species (E form) was calculated as the sum of E int and the deformation energy (E def), which is the increase in energy due to the deformation of the EC or FEC during formation of the complex [29]. E def was calculated at the MP2/6-311G** level. The electrostatic and induction energies were calculated using ORIENT version 3.2 [30]. The electrostatic energy of the complex was calculated as interactions between the distributed multipoles of the molecules. Distributed multipoles [31,32] up to hexadecapole on all atoms were obtained from the MP2/6-311G** wave functions of an isolated molecule using the GDMA program [33]. Induction energy was calculated as the interactions of polarizable sites with the electric field produced by the distributed multipoles of monomers [34]. The atomic polarizabilities of carbon (a = 9 au), oxygen (a = 7 au), and fluorine (a = 2.5 au) were used for the calculations. Distributed multipoles were used only to estimate the electrostatic and induction energies.

MD simulations of the Li[FSA] solutions were performed using the MPDyn2 program [35] under the NTP ensemble condition controlled by the Nosé–Hoover chain thermostat [36–38] and Andersen barostat [39], respectively (at 403 K and 0.1 MPa). Time constants for the thermostat and the barostat were 0.5 ps and 2 ps, respectively. All C–H bonds were held rigid using the SHAKE algorithm [40] and the Coulombic interactions were estimated by Ewald method [41] under periodic boundary conditions where the nonbonded forces were valid up to 12 Å. The time step size for updating interactions in the Ewald reciprocal space was 8 fs and that for other interactions was 2 fs. After a 1 ns equilibration run, an 8 ns production run was performed at 403 K to calculate the radial distribution functions (RDFs) and the mean-square displacements (MSDs). To minimize possible artifacts in the initial configurations, the system was equilibrated at 453 K starting from a low-density condition before equilibrations at 403 K. We utilized the OPLS-AA-based polarizable force field for the simulations. The isotropic atomic induced dipole model was used for the polarizable force field. The details of the force field are shown in the supplementary information (see SI, Tables S3–S7). The composition (i.e., the number of Li[FSA] ion pairs, and EC and FEC molecules) in a cubic cell was as follows: Li[FSA]/EC = 100/150 and Li[FSA]/FEC = 100/150. Trajectory data were collected every 0.2 ps for the analyses and MPDyn2 was used for all the trajectory analyses. The self-diffusion coefficient was obtained from the slope of the linear fitting for MSD versus time in the range of 1–2 ns.

The solvent polarity parameters, δ 23Na, DN NMR and dielectric constant (ε r) of EC and FEC are listed in Table 1 . For liquid electrolytes, Li+ and the solvent act as the Lewis acid and base, respectively. The solvation power or Lewis basicity of the solvents can be characterised by the Gutmann's donor number. In this study, the donor number was estimated as DN NMR on the basis of the empirical linear relationship of the donor number with δ 23Na (Eq. (3)). The DN NMR value of FEC (7.9) is lower than that of EC (11.6), indicating that FEC is more weakly coordinating solvent. In a previous study, the DN NMR value of bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)amide ([TFSA]−) anions was estimated as 8–10 in aprotic ionic liquids [23]. Therefore, the donor property of the counter anion of the Li salt ([FSA]−), which is a structural analogue of [TFSA]−, was considered comparable to that of FEC but lower than that of EC. Another solvent polarity parameter, ε r, is crucial for discussing ion–ion interactions or the degree of salt dissociation in liquid electrolytes. The value of ε r was high for both FEC (79.9) and EC (88.9) among the organic solvents.

Fig. 1 shows the optimized geometries and intermolecular interaction energies (E int) of the [Li(EC)]+ and [Li(FEC)]+ complexes. The contribution of the Coulombic interactions between Li+ and the solvents in the complexes was further evaluated with the electrostatic (E es) and induction (E ind) interactions, as summarized in Table 2 .

The E int for the [Li(FEC)]+ complex (−46.2 kcal mol−1) is smaller (less negative) than that for the [Li(EC)]+ complex (−50.5 kcal mol−1), clearly indicating that EC interacts with Li+ more strongly than FEC. The electrostatic and induction energies for [Li(FEC)]+ are −38.5 and −22.4 kcal mol−1, respectively. These values are smaller (less negative) than those for [Li(EC)]+ (−43.1 and −22.8 kcal mol−1). The fluorine atom of FEC mitigates the electrostatic and induction interactions with Li+ owing to its strong electronegativity. Indeed, the negative charge on the carbonyl oxygen atom of FEC is smaller than that of EC. The atomic charges of the carbonyl oxygen atoms of FEC and EC obtained by electrostatic potential fitting are −0.46 and −0.51 e, respectively. The other atomic charges obtained by the fitting are shown in Fig. S4.
Purple: Li+, red: O, grey: C, light grey: H and sky blue: F.

The physicochemical properties and diffusivity data of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] at 30 °C are listed in Table 3 . HCEs can be prepared with a minimum solvent/Li salt molar ratio of 1.5 for both EC and FEC without coagulation and their Li salt concentration (c) is >5 mol dm−3. The samples with lower solvent/Li salt molar ratios solidified at room temperature and were excluded from this study. [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] shows higher ionic conductivity (1.02 mS cm−1) and lower viscosity (306 mPa s) than [Li(EC)1.5][FSA].

The self-diffusion coefficients of Li and the solvent in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] are nearly identical (D Li ≈ D Sol) and larger than that of [FSA]− as shown in Table 3; however, the absolute value of D Li is much smaller than that of conventional organic liquid electrolytes. In general, the order of the magnitude of the self-diffusion coefficients is D Li < D anion < D Sol in organic electrolyte solutions at a concentration of 1 mol dm−3 or below [43]. In typical electrolyte solutions, D Li < D Sol is due to the larger hydrodynamic radius of “solvated” Li+ compared to those of anions and solvents based on the Stokes-Einstein relation. For [Li(EC)1.5][FSA], the apparent hydrodynamic radii of Li and EC molecules are smaller than that of [FSA]− according to the order of self-diffusion coefficients: D FSA<D Li~D Sol. Here, D FSA<D Li implies that [FSA]− exists as neutral ion pairs and/or larger ion aggregates (Li m [FSA] n ) m-n . In addition, D Li~D Sol indicates that Li+ forms a relatively long-lived complex with EC and diffuses together with EC as the EC–Li[FSA] complex similar to the reported glyme-based molten salt solvates [2,44]. Therefore, the ion pair and the ion aggregates were considered to coexist in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA].

For [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA], the order of the self-diffusion coefficients was D FSA<D Li<D Sol and the absolute values of the self-diffusion coefficients were higher than those of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] owing to the lower viscosity. D FSA<D Li is similar to that of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA], and [FSA]− would coordinate to Li+ to form ion pairs and/or ion aggregates. In contrast to [Li(EC)1.5][FSA], D Sol was slightly higher than D Li in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA], suggesting that FEC molecules remain uncoordinated to some extent and the solvent exchange between the complexes is more frequent.

These results can be attributed to the well-balanced Lewis acid–base interactions between the Li+-solvent and Li+-[FSA]− in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] as suggested by the comparable DN NMR values of FEC and [FSA]−. A similar behavior was observed in glyme-based HCEs with balanced interactions of multidentate glymes and strongly Lewis basic anions, such as trifluoroacetate ([TFA]−) and NO3 −, showing D Li < D Sol and short residence time of the glyme solvents [2,45].

Given the relatively high ionic conductivities of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA], the presumed ion pairs, ion aggregates, and complexes in the HCEs are unlikely to be persistent and rather transient, allowing rapid ion/solvent exchange between these clusters. The lower viscosity and higher ionic conductivity of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] are attributed to the more pronounced ion/solvent exchange in the HCE with a weakly coordinating solvent.

Raman spectroscopy was performed for the EC- and FEC-based electrolytes to investigate the local Li+ coordination structure more in detail. As shown in Fig. 2 , the bands at approximately 1220–1240 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of S=O in [FSA]−, which is sensitive to the Li+–[FSA]− interactions in the electrolytes [46]. Although there is an overlap of the small bands attributed to CH2 twist vibration [47] of EC (Fig. 2 (a)) or FEC (Fig. 2 (b)), the effects of these band overlaps were marginal in the highly concentrated region.

For [Li(FEC or EC)3][FSA] and [Li(FEC or EC)4][FSA] mixtures, the broad Raman band can be assigned to [FSA]− engaged in SSIPs (solvent separated ion pairs)/CIPs (contact ion pairs) and non-coordinated [FSA]− [48]. With increasing salt concentration further to [Li(FEC or EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC or EC)2][FSA], the Raman peak positions shifted to higher wavenumbers. The peak position, for instance, was largely shifted from 1222 cm−1 to 1232 cm−1 for [Li(EC)1.5][FSA]. Such significant high-frequency shifts of this band were assigned to the formation of ion aggregates (AGGs) in trimethyl phosphate-based HCEs [48]. Therefore, AGGs are largely present in both [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA].

To further study the Li+ coordination structure, we performed molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of mixtures of Li[FSA] with EC or FEC. The MD simulations were performed at a higher temperature (403 K) than the experimental temperature (303 K) to lower the computational cost: an extremely long time was required to estimate the diffusion coefficients due to the high viscosity of the electrolytes at 303 K. Self-diffusion coefficients of Li+, FSA− and solvents (EC and FEC) in each electrolyte were calculated from MSDs shown in Fig. S1. The calculated self-diffusion coefficients are listed in Table 4 .

The calculated D values were one order of magnitude higher than the experimental D values due to the higher temperature for the calculations. The order of the self-diffusion coefficients calculated for [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] was D FSA < D Li < D Sol. The diffusion of Li+ is faster than that of [FSA]−, which agrees well with that obtained by PFG-NMR measurements. However, for [Li(EC)1.5][FSA], D Sol was larger than D Li in MD simulations at 403 K while D Sol is nearly identical to D Li in the diffusivity measurement at 303 K. Probably, the Li+-solvent interaction becomes weaker at higher temperatures in the MD simulations, leading to D Li < D Sol in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA]. Nevertheless, the difference between D Sol and D Li was smaller for [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] compared to that for [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. Therefore, the difference in the Li+-solvent interactions for [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] was reflected in the MSDs, and the dynamics and liquid structures were sufficiently reproduced in the MD simulations.

Fig. 3 shows the site–site radial distribution functions between Li+ and the carbonyl oxygen atoms of the solvents (EC or FEC) and the oxygen atoms of [FSA]−. The accumulative coordination number for each pair in the range 0–4 Å; the entire range (0–15 Å) of the RDFs is shown in Fig. S2. The MD simulations showed that the coordination of Li+ to the C–O–C oxygen atoms of EC and FEC is negligible as shown in Fig. S3. The RDFs for Li–O[FSA] has a sharp peak at 1.9 Å in both electrolytes. This peak can be assigned to the contact between Li+ and sulfonyl oxygens (SO2) in [FSA]−. Sharp peaks were also observed in the RDFs of Li–OEC and Li–OFEC for carbonyl oxygen atoms (C=O) at the same distance (1.9 Å). The RDFs for Li–OEC and FEC (C–O–C) have broad weak peaks at approximately 3–4 Å, as shown in Fig. S3. The distances for these peaks coincide with the distance between the Li and oxygen atom of C–O–C in the optimized geometries of the [Li(EC)]+ and [Li(FEC)]+ complexes as illustrated in Fig. 1. These results show that the coordination of the oxygen atom in cyclic C–O–C to Li+ is negligible.

The accumulated coordination numbers of Li+ with anions and solvents in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] are shown in Fig. 3. The coordination numbers of oxygen atoms of EC and [FSA] at 3.0 Å in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] were 1.38 and 3.01, respectively. This corroborates that almost all EC molecules (92%) coordinated to Li+ to form an EC-Li[FSA] complex in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] as discussed with the experimental diffusivity (D Li~D Sol, Table 3). In addition, three of four oxygen atoms of [FSA]− (75%) bound to Li+, suggesting the formation of CIPs and AGGs in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA], indicating the formation of CIPs and AGGs in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA], consistent with the diffusivity (D FSA < D Li, Table 3) and the Raman spectra of the stretching vibration of S=O in [FSA]− (Fig. 2). The larger DN NMR value of EC than [FSA]− is responsible for the larger fraction of the coordinated oxygen atoms of EC than that of [FSA]−.

The coordination numbers of oxygen atoms of FEC and [FSA] at 3.0 Å in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] were 1.23 and 3.24, respectively. The majority of FEC (82%) was involved in the complexes, but there was a slightly larger content of the uncoordinated and/or highly exchangeable solvent in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. This can be ascribed to the weaker intermolecular interaction energies (E int) for FEC, as estimated by the ab initio calculations (Fig. 1). Alternatively, the interaction between [FSA]− and Li+ in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] was slightly stronger than that in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA], which is reflected in the increase in the coordination number of [FSA]− from 3.01 ([Li(EC)1.5][FSA]) to 3.24 ([Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]). The fraction of the coordinated oxygen atoms was almost the same value for FEC (82%) and [FSA]− (81%) in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. This would be ascribed to the comparable DN NMR values of FEC and [FSA]−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: t Li PP and Onsager transport coefficients
ce:section.ce:para:
The Li+ transference number was obtained by the potentiostatic polarization method (t Li PP) [49,50], owing to the convenience of evaluating the Li+ transport properties assuming the actual electrochemical devices. The t Li PP also referred as the “current fraction” [51], is defined as shown in the following equation:
(4)
t Li PP
=
I SS
V DC
−
I Ohm
R
i , 0
I Ohm
V DC
−
I SS
R
i , SS
where V DC is a constant applied voltage, I Ohm and I SS are the initial and steady-state currents, and R i, 0 and R i, SS are the initial and steady-state interfacial resistance values, respectively. The initial current was calculated using Ohm's law, I Ohm = V DC/(R bulk + R i, 0). In addition, t Li PP is intimately related to the ion dynamics, as mentioned in the Introduction, and can be expressed by the Onsager coefficients (see Eqs. (1) and (2)) [15,16].

Table 5 shows t Li PP, α and β of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] and the Onsager coefficients (σ ++, σ −−, and σ +−) required for the calculation of the parameters α and β were summarized in Table S2. Interestingly, [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] exhibits the higher t Li PP value of 0.73 than that of 0.57 for [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and the difference of t Li PP value can be ascribed to the interactions between Li+ and the solvents. Both HCEs showed approximately zero values of β (0.08 and 0.06 for [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA], respectively), presumably due to high ε r of the solvent and dissociability of Li[FSA]. This clearly indicates the nearly non-correlated cation-anion motions (σ +−~ 0) in the HCEs. Therefore, the ion pairs, aggregates, and Li-solvent complexes observed in Raman study and MD simulation are verified to be transient in both [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA], enabling the rapid reformation of one clusters to another.

In contrast to the parameter β, α for [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] (0.68) is larger than that of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] (0.54). The higher α value indicates a more significant contribution of Li+ to the ion conduction, and is directly involved in the higher t Li PP value of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. The difference in the parameter α of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] will be associated with the rate of Li+/solvent exchange and the residence time of solvents for Li+ in the HCEs. For instance, short-chain glyme-based HECs, [Li(G1)2][TFSA] and [Li(G1)1(G2)1][TFSA], showed a significantly large α value (0.88 for [Li(G1)2][TFSA] and 0.93 for [Li(G1)1(G2)1][TFSA]) [18], and their residence time of solvents for Li+ was much shorter than that of HCEs with long-chain multidentate ligands [15] owing to their lower stability for the [Li(glyme)]+ complexes [52]. Indeed, the calculated E int for [Li(FEC)]+ was much smaller (less negative) than that for [Li(EC)]+. A larger content of the liberative solvents was suggested by the experimental diffusivity data (D Li < D Sol), and the lower solvation number of the solvent in the MD simulation for [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. These results imply that more efficient Li+ transport (higher α value) can be attributable to more rapid and frequent Li+/solvent exchange and shorter residence time of the solvent in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. Considering the above analysis for α and β based on the Onsager transport coefficients, it is conceivable that the concept of HCEs with weakly coordinating solvents and Li salts is an effective approach to achieve high t Li PP value.

The reductive and oxidative stabilities of [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] were evaluated by CV and LSV using three-electrode cells. Both [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] exhibited the reversible Li metal deposition and dissolution at 0.19 V and 0.21 V vs. the reference (Li/Li+ in 1 mol dm−3 Li[TFSA]/PC), respectively (Fig. 4 ). The Li deposition/dissolution potential shifted to a positive potential (vs. the reference) because of the Nernst shift in the HCEs with high Li salt concentration [53]. Thus, this remarkable positive shift of the Li/Li+ electrode potential suggests that Li+ is more energetically unstable in the HCEs than the reference solution and even more unstable for [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. The more labile Li+ would contribute to more rapid and frequent Li+/solvent exchange in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] as discussed in the previous section.

As seen in Fig. 4, the deposition/dissolution current density remained unchanged in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] for 15 cycles, but gradually decreased with repeated cycles in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. Moreover, the Coulombic efficiency for Li deposition/dissolution was lower in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] (53% at the 15th cycle) than that in [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] (70% at the 15th cycle). These results suggest more significant side reactions in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. From ab initio molecular orbital calculations for the solvents, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels were calculated to be 0.659 and 0.708 eV for FEC and EC, respectively. The lower LUMO energy level of FEC is primarily responsible for the lower reductive stability of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA].

Fig. 5 shows linear sweep voltammograms on a Pt working electrode. The two electrolytes showed relatively high oxidative stabilities, and their oxidative current was negligible at potential E < 4 V vs. the reference. However, the oxidative current becomes clearly higher for [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] above 4.5 V. In a previous work, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level of solvents was found to be lowered by the formation of the Li+-solvent complex, and the scarcity of the uncoordinated solvents was responsible for high oxidative stability of HCEs [54]. Similarly, HOMO energy level of the carbonate solvents was significantly reduced to the lower level for the corresponding Li+-solvent complexes: −13.57 eV for FEC and − 18.16 eV for [Li(FEC)]+; −12.96 eV for EC and − 17.38 eV for [Li(EC)]+. The HOMO energy levels of the FEC-based electrolyte are lower than those of the EC-based electrolyte due to electron-withdrawing F atom in FEC. However, as suggested by the diffusivity measurements and MD simulations, there are relatively larger content of the liberated solvents in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. The preferential decomposition of these solvent species is considered to lead to the lower oxidative stability of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA].

Fig. 6 illustrates the cycle performance of discharge capacity and discharge curves of Li/LCO cells with different current densities in the range 0.4–5.0 mA cm−2 (1 C = 0.64 mA cm−2) at 30 °C. Here, the rate capability test was performed at constant and low current density (0.13 mA cm−2) for the charging process to avoid Li dendrite formation at the Li metal counter electrode during cycling. At lower current densities (< 1 mA cm−2), the difference in the discharge capacity was not apparent between the cells with [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]; both cells retained the practical capacity of ~140 mAh g−1 (Fig. 6 (a)). However, at higher current densities, > 2 mA cm−2, the cell with [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] delivered a higher capacity (122 mAh g−1) even at 5 mA cm−2 with a high Coulombic efficiency (~ 99.8%), whereas the discharge capacity and Coulombic efficiency were decreased by 75 mAh g−1 and 95.3%, respectively, for the cell with [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. In the discharge curves (Fig. 6 (b) and (c)), the discharge overpotential at higher current densities (3 and 5 mA cm−2) is significantly larger for the cell with [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. Given the transport properties (t Li PP and σ ion) of the HCEs, the lower rate capability and the large overpotential of the cell with [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] is a seemingly paradoxical result. A possible interpretation is that the reductive and oxidative decomposition of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] deteriorated the cell during the charge-discharge cycles. Indeed, the cell with [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] showed lower Coulombic efficiencies and gradual decay in the discharge capacity at higher current densities (Fig. 6 (a)).

The electrode potential of the LCO electrode in the HCEs would shift to a positive potential in parallel with the observed potential shift of the Li deposition/dissolution reactions (Fig. 4). The cut-off potential for charging of the battery test (4.2 V vs. Li counter electrode in the HCEs) thus converted to 4.41 V for [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] vs. the reference used in CV and LSV measurements (Li/Li+ in 1 mol dm−3 Li[TFSA]/PC). As shown in Fig. 5, a small oxidative current was detected in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] at this potential. Therefore, the oxidative decomposition of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] during charging can be one of possible reasons for the deterioration of the cell with [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. The lower reductive stability of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] at the Li counter electrode, as suggested by CV measurements (Fig. 4), would also be the cause of the inferior rate performance of the cell with [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. Consequently, the lower electrochemical stability of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] adversely affected the charge-discharge performance at high current densities even though [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] showed higher σ ion and t Li PP.

The effects of Li+-solvent interactions on the ionic transport and electrochemical properties were investigated through a comparative study of [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(EC)1.5][FSA]. The weaker Li+–FEC interaction characterized by low DN NMR and E int values was reflected in a slightly larger content of the uncoordinated and/or highly exchangeable solvent in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. Although the formation of the ion pairs, AGGs and the Li-solvent complexes was confirmed in both HCEs by Raman spectroscopy and MD simulations, the nearly zero values of β strongly support that these ion clusters are not persistent but transient. The significant positive potential shift (0.21 V) of the Li deposition/dissolution reaction suggests that more labile Li+ was formed in [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. The larger α value proves a more pronounced contribution of Li+ to ion conduction in the weakly coordinating [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. Therefore, the presence of more liberative solvents and more labile Li+ can be involved in rapid ion/solvent exchange, leading to lower viscosity and higher σ ion and t Li PP for [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]. However, the weaker Li+–FEC interaction was found to have adverse effects on the electrochemical stability of HCE. [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA] exhibited lower reductive and oxidative stabilities due to the intrinsically lower LUMO level of the solvent and the preferential oxidative decomposition of the more liberative FEC, respectively. At high current densities, the Li/LiCoO2 cell with [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] showed discharge capacity decay with lower Coulombic efficiency due to electrolyte decomposition under the electrode potential with inevitable Nernst shift. The introduction of weaker Li-solvent interactions into HCEs compromised the electrochemical stability while effectively enhancing t Li and σ ion. The higher transport properties based on the weaker Li-solvent interactions would allow greater rate capability in a battery with lower operating voltage, where the electrolyte degradation is negligible. However, for high-voltage Li-ion batteries, further optimization of the liquid electrolyte is needed considering the electrochemical stability in addition to the enhancement of the transport properties.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported in part by the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) KAKENHI (Grant No. 20H02837 to K. U., 18H03926 to S. T., and 18H03926 to K. D.), JST ALCA-SPRING (Grant No. JPMJAL1301) and Asahi Glass Foundation, Japan. This study is also based on results obtained from a project, JPNP20004, subsidized by the New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization (NEDO).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The details of the Onsager transport coefficients are provided. The mean-square displacements and additional RDF data were summarized in the MD simulation. All numerical data for EC and FEC: densities (both experimental and MD simulations) and [Li(EC)1.5][FSA] and [Li(FEC)1.5][FSA]: the Onsager transport coefficients (σ ++, σ −−, and σ +−) are summarized in Table S2. The bond stretching, angle bending, torsional, improper and nonbonding parameters for the MD simulations were summarized. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nocx.2021.100071.
